turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This case is a multi-facet case which arose out of the 8th Circuit. It concerns the proposition as to whether or not collateral estoppel applies when a prisoner seeks to invoke the jurisdiction of the Federal District Court under 42 United States Code Section 1983. In this particular case the 8th Circuit held that because of the special role of the federal courts in protecting civil rights and because habeas corpus is now unavailable to However, this particular Act has been somewhat modi­ are able to rule constitutional issues involving federal constitutional law, and that some trust has to be placed in state courts concerning this issue. Going back to the Civil Rights Act, I believe that in this day and age things have drastically changed and that state courts are applying constitutional law, federal constitutional law, and are complying with all decisions of this particular Court, especially in the role of search and seizure.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Fitzgibbon, can I ask you a question about the procedural posture of this case? As I understand it the respondent in this case was convicted in the trial court, or his motion to suppress was denied, and he had an appeal evidence. They were aware of that particular decision and it

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And did the case go to the Missouri Supreme Court, Mr. Fitzgibbon?

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: No, it did not. It is limited on this particular issue to the Missouri Court of Appeals so that there was no further --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could he have gone to the Missouri Supreme Court?

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could the defendant have taken his convic­ tion to the Missouri Supreme Court?

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: No, Your Honor, not under our law. It would have to stop at the Missouri Court of Appeals, and it

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Was cert, applied for?

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: I am not familiar with that particu­ lar point because I am not the prosecutor in the case, I repre­ sent the police officers. Whether or not cert, was applied for or not I really don't know, in this particular case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: When you speak of cert, are you speaking of cert, to the highest court in Missouri?

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: No, sir. Certiorari to the Supreme Court of the United States. I am not familiar as to whether or not that's —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, certiorari from the Missouri Court of Appeals which for this case is the highest court in Missouri. convicted upon constitutional standards of search and seizure and therefore his conviction should be affirmed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that turned on federal law?

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: On federal law, and they so cited the federal rules concerning that in federal cases.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Couldn't that issue have been taken to the Missouri Supreme Court?

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: I guess you'd have to go back to the Civil Rights Act to find out what the effect was and whe­ ther or not collateral estoppel should apply. The federal rules, claims should not be heard by courts. In this particular instance, I think there is no doubt that the common law always did recognize that collateral estoppel will apply, that if you have had a fair and full hearing on an issue, in my words, you only get one bite at the apple, the idea being to give some finality to litigation. It has to stop somewhere. Now, the issue in this particular case is if the prisoner in this particular case was given a full and fair hearing in the state court's suppression of the evidence case. Does he now have a right, notwithstanding the fact that it has been shown by the Missouri Court of Appeals that the search was legal and constitutional, can he now go to the Federal District Court on a petition for damages and relitigate that asked the court for partial summary judgment on the issue that we're here on today, and the court, I guess inadvertently, granted me full summary judgment, which I really didn-'t ask for. I was willing to go to trial with the assault.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that may have to go back?

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: That -- yes; yes. That probably will be tried.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That was remanded by the Court of Appeals?

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: That was remanded and that issue should be tried. I didn't address that issue; I wanted to get this issue into the eye of the Court so they could rule on this particular thing in our Circuit. Now, some of the cases have made distinctions on were before Stone v. Powell, and therefore, since Stone v. Powell has taken away habeas corpus on Fourth Amendment viola­ tions, virtually, that therefore we have to have a federal foruir and some of these cases go off on the theory, well, since it’s a criminal case, the prisoner didn't ask to be in the courtroom and the prisoner didn't really ask to have these particular issues decided but he was forced to be there and he was forced to bring up these issues -- and they take off on a tack saying, well, if he wants to have a 1983 action for damages, then he can't litigate that in the state court, he's going to get con­ victed because he's not going to bring that up. I think that's absolutely false. If a person is in court and is going, may have to do assaulted him while he was lying on the ground with his hands behind his back. The two officers that he shot, of course, didn't have anything to do with the particular assault because they were immediately, got medical aid and they were taken off to the hospital. So it would be a third officer who he identi­ fied in his cross petition for summary judgment. I don't think that the test is whether an issue is decided, whether you're there voluntarily or involuntarily. I think that the test that should be applied in state court proceedings is to whether or not he had a full and fair oppor­ this judgment in the criminal case defensively?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you wouldn't say that a plaintiff

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: He could go back, if the issue is decided in his favor, certainly, under 1983.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: We have different parties then.

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: It could go in. I don't think the courts have distinguished in collateral estoppel or what they call issue preclusion; or — you have three things --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: When the police officers had never had an opportunity to litigate and argue in good faith and so forth? That could be precluded.

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: As I understand the question, if it trial it was predetermined there was not probable cause. Would

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: It would be open in a 1983 suit?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: Or he could proceed to trial, in a jury trial against the police officers. It's not preclusive upon the police officers.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But typically, in your criminal trial, a motion to suppress may be granted notwithstanding the good faith immunity of the officers in a 1983 action, and if there were then, the motion to suppress were granted, wouldn't that be conclusive as to the violation of the constitutional rights in a 1983 action, so that the only matter open would be the good faith of the officers? dence found in the drawer and in a tire?

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And suppose they have therefore decided as a matter of collateral estoppel that to that extent there was a violation of Fourth Amendment rights?

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: No, Your Honor, because the whole posture of this particular case was based on a conspiracy of the two officers to conduct an illegal search, the two officers that were shot by this prisoner.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And we have a state court adjudication that there was some violation of the Fourth Amendment rights in this in the case. But that is not the particular issue in this case. The posture is that he started, he alleges that our two police officers conspired to illegally search his home.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I know it's really not relevant but I have some problems with your damage theory that you're going to, that they're going to wrestle with if they win; but --

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: If you're talking about the stuff which was suppressed, it's narcotics. What are his --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm trying to understand if it was not suppressed, if they -- I would hate to be the trial judge trying to decide how to apportion, how to award damages if you ulti­ mately lose. on that particular issue --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Under Mincey?

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: And said -- yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh, did it?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Which court of appeals are you referring to now?

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: I'm referring to the Missouri Court of Appeals, not to the 8th Circuit. The 8th Circuit just didn't even get into the issue as to whether or not these facts show that there was an illegal search and seizure.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do we have that opinion of the -- I was to him or is it --

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: You can't give marijuana back to somebody. It's contraband; it has to be destroyed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's what I was thinking.

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: If you want to get into the issue of what was the street value of the heroin, and that's the ques­ tion of damages, I don't see how we're going to reach that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It might not impress a jury very much as as an amount of damages.

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: I don't think so. I don't think so.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Fitzgibbon, is this the Missouri Court of Appeals opinion at Appendix C of the petition?

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. evidence, whether or not this evidence was seized legally or illegally. It seems that if the exclusionary rule were abolished I myself would be willing to defend each and every police offi­ cer on a damage claim. It appears that the exclusionary rule doesn't really accomplish that much. The deterrent effect of that is placed upon society when I think the deterrent effect should really be placed upon a police officer, the police offi­ cer who does -- say, intentionally -- violate somebody's rights by an illegal search and seizure. It seems to me that the onus of that should be placed upon the police officer in an action for damages and that the that particular law and the congressional reports on this show any indication that they wish to abolish collateral estoppel.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, historically, hasn't collateral estoppel required some mutuality or not?

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: I believe the courts have gone fur­ ther, and mutuality -- you mean the mutuality of the same parties and the same issues?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: No, that would be res judicata. But they talk of collateral estoppel where the issue is put into focus and there's a full trial, full, fair-hearing r.~ on that, that that should be collateral estoppel on this par­ ticular case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let me ask you, do you know what the result 342 US which strikes me as being very much on point with your before the evidence was introduced and it was an effort to enjoin the use by the prosecution of the evidence in the crimi­ nal case .

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: I'm not familiar with that case, Your Honor. I guess under the doctrine of abstention they woulc allow, should allow the state courts to continue their criminal prosecution and the criminal processes against --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They surely have them in adequate remedy by way of a motion to suppress, have they not?

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: There's an adequate remedy in the state court to do so, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Fitzgibbon, assuming that the defendant the same evidence was required --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or involving the same search.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In which your question involved the same search; right.

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: I think that the state decision on constitutional law would be binding. I think the states do have the power.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then we're really not talking about the construction of 1983, because if you take the position that it would apply in a federal criminal prosecution, it's a somewhat broader concept.

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm not saying it's wrong, but it's not

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Which is really what the 8th Circuit limited this exception to, as I understand it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, on that formulation, then, it's both. It's also a 1983 statutory construction problem to the extent that you construe 1983 as not intending an exception.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN J. FITZGIBBON
turns.text: As I say, in conclusion, I would be willing to defend any police officer concerning illegal search and seizure and give them, if the Court needs some remedy for these persons who claim that their Fourth Amendment constitu­ tional rights were violated and action for damages for this rather than excluding the evidence of any particular criminal for no rehearing. We directly petitioned this Court for cer­

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Shank. ORAL ARGUMENT BY JEFFREY J. SHANK

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I think it particularly appropriate at this time, possibly, to review briefly the factual setting for clarifica­ tion purposes. Respondent filed his civil rights action alleging three general areas, one,the assault matter, which has been discussed with the Court; secondly, he raised a search question in two areas, one, a conspiracy situation and secondly alleging more than the defendant had asked.

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: That's correct, sir. And neither did he — it appeared the Court might have been ruling on the motion to dismiss and used the terminology under the summary judgment. That's unclear. The District Court said that the only issue that it had was whether or not the entrance into the respondent's home was lawful and that this was litigated in state court. The state court in the criminal proceedings at the trial level held, one, that a portion of the evidence should be suppressed, and a portion of it was not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That was the contraband drugs, is that correct, that were excluded?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Were any of these defendants involved in those searches?

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: They would have been involved. As to the conspiracy claimed it's quite possible that the named officers Jacobsmeyer and Allen would have been involved in the conspiracy to violate the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Dealing with the suppressed items?

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: Correct, sir. Also, as to the -- we also pled, or the respondent who was pro se pled that there were unknown defendants. We subsequently learned the names of those particular defendants, and the 8th Circuit gave us leave to amend the assault claim and we would also think it would be of Appeals decision come down? Approximately?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: August 14, 1979.

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: In '79, in the Missouri Court of Appeals. in the -- finding of the circumstances, the fact that the respondent in the state case — defendant there — was really compelled to raise those issues there --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you recall, it used to be that -- until we overruled it -- that under Darr and Burford you couldn't go into the federal habeas unless you first sought cert, in this Court. But I guess it's never been the rule that you could not come, start a 1983 action without first exhausting by application for cert, with this Court.

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: To my knowledge there is no exhaustion requirement for 1983 action.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: When was the damage action against the police officers commenced, approximately again? Before or after dent filed the pro se motion and certainly would have sought assistance of counsel, I'm sure, at that time.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Shank, your office did not represent him in the state court proceedings?

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: No, sir, we were appointed by the 8th Circuit Court of Appeals to represent him on the appeal from the Federal District Court's decision.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How can you assume that counsel, that the client wouldn't tell the counsel he had a case pending? How can you assume that he would not do that?

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: Well, my sole —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I know that they don't want to tell the truth but I mean -- MR. SHANK Yes .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: My question is, isn't that a duty of counsel to tell the court that some other court is hearing this same matter? MR. SHANK The Federal District Court?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Any court.

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: Any court?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Any court. Don't you think that's a duty to tell the court that?

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: Under our interpretation of 1983, it would not be because we would -- know about it. MR. SHANK I understand that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And whose duty was it to notify them?

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: I understand what the Court is saying.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you were not counsel at the trial level, as I understand it?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, he wasn't. MR. SHANK No, sir, nor at the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You were neither in the state court nor -- was he represented at all in the state court?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm not saying it about you because I knew you weren't there. MR. SHANK In the state trial court he was repre- Appeals of Missouri judgment affirming the conviction, isn't right that -- the fact that this Court is open to remedy erroneous rulings on search and seizure? And if you allow a 1983 action in every case where Stone v. Powell bars habeas, you're pretty well overruling Stone v. Powell.

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: Well, sir, in the Stone v. Powell matter I believe the Court indicated that if a state court provided a full and fair hearing or opportunity to raise the Fourth Amendment issues, the habeas corpus would not be an available remedy to a respondent or defendant. Our feeling is that it's -• the federal court in reviewing that would only be reviewing a due process situation. It would not be reviewing the substan­ tive application of the constitutional rights, whether or not

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: Well, I feel that the reviewing court, if we use the full and fair opportunity kind of test, would only be reviewing whether or not there was a hearing on the issue, whether or not their evidence was presented, and that type of situation. But I don't believe the reviewing court's going to look at how the law was applied to the facts. It's quite possi­ ble that the state court could apply law that's contradictory to the present status of the federal —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, then, doesn't Stone v. Powell say, you petition for certiorari here rather, because relief is not available on habeas corpus.

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: So it would be as a matter -- you could proceed under the habeas corpus. Of course, if there was a habeas; yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Of course, the other side of this coin that Mr. Justice Rehnquist has raised is that if collateral arbiter.

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: Correct. That way there would be no viable federal forum to vindicate the Fourth Amendment rights.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think it's relevant at all whether where 1983 was adopted in 1871 -- collateral estoppel had a requirement of mutuality? Do you think it's relevant at all to our decision here? If we're dealing with a construction of 1983 and if where 1983 was required collateral estoppel was not recognized except in sitations of mutuality, does that apply here?

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: Our position would be, no, but we're not -- we go beyond that to clearly -- the two-prong test that petitioner suggested, whether or not a common law application would be applied, is that assuming, which it was, mutuality be arguably the worst possible result? It says, in effect, for the Federal District Court not to intervene in 1983 until the matter has gone all the way through the state courts, but then it may intervene.

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that if you're talking about considera­ tions of federalism or comity, perhaps there is a greater affront to the state systems if the federal court does not intervene until it's, say, gone to the highest court of the state, and then the federal court decides differently than the highest court of the state.

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: But we feel that we would not be inter­ courts similar to this suit here, it wouldn't be thrown out on -- or there would be no mutuality requirement?

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: Correct, sir, and we would have a set of circumstances similar to that's before this Court, the defen­ dant in that case could raise the doctrine of collateral estoppel quite possibly.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what would the Missouri -- well, and what do you think the state courts would say? What would they decide ?

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: Would they apply collateral estoppel? I think the state court probably would. That's enough -- that's the whole reason for the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you don't really know. The state

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Historically, don't you understand that only a relatively modern development? Civil Rights Act of 1871 was passed, mutuality did exist.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you know when the first Missouri case was that didn't require mutuality?

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: No, sir, I don't. I'd be glad to brief that to the Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Shank, I take it from what you say that if instead of having a Fourth Amendment issue you had a Fifth -- say you were bringing a 1983 case on the theory that a confession had been extorted by force or violence, would collateral estoppel apply in that situation?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And under the decision now before it.

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: . Correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's not your view that it ought to be?

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: No, sir, my view would be that the legis- lative history is clear that 1983 precludes the application of collateral estoppel in any circumstance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I take it you don't regard this Court as a federal forum?

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: Oh, yes; yes, sir. I'm just saying that the opportunity at the time when the State Court of Appeals has ruled and this respondent in this particular case had, we can assume, a choice into which court he could proceed, I feel that it could have proceeded here or it could have proceeded into Federal District Court. litigation of civil rights under the Civil Rights Act, under those circumstances the Civil Rights Act for all intents and purposes would be dead in the majority of the cases.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what do you do with Stefanelli v. Minard, which refused to enjoin the use of evidence in a state court even though the contention was that it had been seized contrary to the Fourth Amendment?

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: Well that, I would say, if it's come to those certain set of circumstances, the Court was merely ab­ staining to let a state court make its determination, then cer­ tainly I don't think that would necessarily have to preclude the litigation of the issue later on once the state court had finished. If I may, to restate the issue we are asking this in August, 1980, ruled that collateral estoppel would be inap­ plicable, and in particular it noted that because there was no right to appeal to the Supreme Court, it felt that the collateral estoppel would not be applicable. All those cases, these latter cases, rely heavily on the legislative history, which clearly show that the Civil Rights Act was intended to --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: We will resume there at 1 o’clock. You have about five minutes remaining.

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: Okay. Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Shank, you may proceed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Shank, just before the noon recess

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: M-a-t-e-e-r, et al. And it's United

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Two-four-eight ?

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what did that case decide?

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: That was decided on August 7, 1980.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what did it decidej what did it hold?

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: It held that collateral estoppel would be inapplicable in a situation as we've presented to this Court. It's on all fours.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In this particular situation, Fourth Amendment claim?

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So you say that's in accord with the 8th

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: In our opinion that is one of the major considerations; yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But is it not true that your basic position is that Stone is immaterial?

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: No, sir. We feel for the purposes of the issue before this Court it is all-encompassing because --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but I think you responded to an earlier question that if this were a Fifth Amendment claim you'd still make the same argument.

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: Yes, sir. Well, we would there have the availability of habeas corpus and we'd have the federal forum.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right, I understand. I'm not talking about that there wasn't a full and fair hearing in the state court and the Federal District Court agrees, holds a new hearing, and finds that there was no violation of the Fourth Amendment and denies the petition for habeas corpus. Then the petitioner who is a defendant in the state court in the criminal action sues on 1983 claiming a violation of the Fourth Amendment, and sues the police officers. The police officers say, collateral estoppel. What would you say?

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: Assuming that the Federal District Court upheld the constitutionality of the search, he would then in that instance have been provided the federal forum that we feel that is a basic requirement for the 1983 action.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you wouldn't claim a -- you would say action could actually -- maybe the statute of limitations would preclude it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, logically, I suppose you'd say, if he had come directly here and we had granted certiorari and we had passed on the Fourth Amendment and we sustained the state court, that then he could not go into 1983 because he had had his federal forum.

turns.name: JEFFREY J. SHANK
turns.text: Under the issue we're asking the Court to decide, yes, sir. Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You wouldn't say that if we had just denied cert.? CERTIFICATE North American Reporting hereby certifies that the attache d pages represent an accurate transcript of electronic sound re­ cording of the oral argument before the Supreme Court of the United States, in the matter of: No. 79-935 Allen v. McCurry and that these pages constitute the original transcript of the proceedings for the records of the Court.
